phrasalVerbs.definition: To make someone or something show a quality that they have; to produce a new product.
examples: The tech giant is bringing out a new smartphone next month.
Competition brings out the best in some athletes.
The company brought out an innovative line of sustainable products.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To gradually develop or increase something over time.
examples: She built up the business from nothing over ten years.
We need to build up our customer base before expanding.
He's been building up his investment portfolio steadily.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To buy large amounts of something or all of it that is available.
examples: Investors are buying up properties in the city centre.
The corporation bought up all its competitors.
Speculators bought up the entire stock of rare coins.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To take something that you earn or are given in one year into the next one.
examples: Unused holiday days can be carried over to next year.
The profits were carried over into the following quarter.
Some tax benefits can be carried over for several years.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To lock the doors of a building or business; to cease operations for the day.
examples: We close up at nine o'clock on weekdays.
The shop owner was closing up when the last customer arrived.
Make sure to close up properly before leaving.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To begin with something; to start an event or activity.
examples: The conference kicks off with a keynote speech tomorrow.
We'll kick off the meeting with a review of last month's figures.
The sale kicks off at midnight.

fillInExercises.sentence: The company is ___ a new line of electric vehicles next year.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Bring out' means to produce and release a new product.

fillInExercises.sentence: She ___ up the business from a small startup to a major corporation.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Build up' means to develop something gradually over time.

fillInExercises.sentence: Investors are ___ real estate across the country.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Buy up' means to purchase large amounts of something.

fillInExercises.sentence: Any unused budget can be ___ over to the next financial year.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Carry over' means to transfer something to a future period.

fillInExercises.sentence: The event will ___ with a welcome reception.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Kick off' means to begin or start something.

fillInExercises.sentence: Don't forget to ___ up the office when you leave tonight.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Close up' means to lock a building or stop operations for the day.

fillInExercises.sentence: Competition ___ the best in creative teams.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Bring out' can also mean to reveal or show a quality.

fillInExercises.sentence: The new marketing campaign ___ off next Monday.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Kick off' means to start or begin something.

lines.speaker: Chen
lines.text: Let's [kick off (with)|kick off (with)] last quarter's performance review.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: 'Kick off' means to begin or start something.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: *kick off (with)* — To begin with something; to start an event or activity.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Let's kick off (with) last quarter's performance review.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not confuse this with other items from the lesson — here you need *kick off (with)* (*kick off (with)*).

wrongOptions.bring out: *bring out* (To make someone or something show a quality that they have) does not match the meaning needed in this line.
wrongOptions.build up: *build up* (To gradually develop or increase something over time) does not match the meaning needed in this line.
wrongOptions.buy up: *buy up* (To buy large amounts of something or all of it that is available) does not match the meaning needed in this line.
wrongOptions.carry over: *carry over* (To take something that you earn or are given in one year into the next one) does not match the meaning needed in this line.
wrongOptions.close up: *close up* (To lock the doors of a building or business) does not match the meaning needed in this line.

explanationContent.usefulTip: In conversation, *kick off (with)* is often used in informal spoken English.
